patient.name: Serena Jackson
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe shortness of breath and chest pain

history.hpi: 35-year-old female, 3 weeks postpartum after an uncomplicated cesarean section, with sudden-onset severe shortness of breath and right-sided pleuritic chest pain that started 30 minutes ago while feeding the baby. Has not been very active since the C-section. No cough, no fever, no leg swelling noted but has been sedentary.
history.events: Sudden-onset severe dyspnea and right pleuritic chest pain while sitting, feeding her newborn. Husband called 911.

scene.dispatch: Medic 8, respond code 3 to 1204 Willow Creek Drive, for a 35-year-old female with difficulty breathing and chest pain, postpartum.
scene.location: Suburban home, main floor living room. Patient seated upright on couch, obviously ill.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Newborn present with husband.
scene.bystanders: Husband, who provides postpartum history. Newborn in a bassinet.
visualCues: Young adult female seated upright, pale and diaphoretic
Obviously distressed, holding the right side of her chest
Cesarean section surgical staples still visible at dressing
No obvious leg swelling on quick inspection
Appears to be in real distress but not altered

phases.name: Arrival — Postpartum Dyspnea with Chest Pain
phases.description: Postpartum patient with sudden onset dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxemia, tachycardia, and hypotension. Clear lungs and risk factor profile raise strong suspicion for massive PE. This is obstructive shock physiology. 12-lead may show S1Q3T3, right heart strain, sinus tachycardia, or a new RBBB.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Rapid at 32/min, shallow, clear bilateral breath sounds; pleuritic pain worsens with inspiration
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak radial pulse, cool peripheries, tachycardic
otherFindings: Clear lung fields — PE presents with clear lungs despite hypoxemia
Postpartum (3 weeks) — significant PE risk factor
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'I can't catch my breath... chest hurts'

expectedActions.action: Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather to target SpO2 ≥94%
Position of comfort — semi-upright as tolerated
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography
Obtain 12-lead EKG looking for sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3 pattern, new RBBB, T wave inversions V1-V4 (right heart strain)
expectedActions.rationale: The 12-lead can support a PE diagnosis with features of right heart strain — key for destination decision.

expectedActions.action: Recognize pattern: postpartum + sudden dyspnea + pleuritic chest pain + hypoxia + hypotension + clear lungs + low EtCO2 = suspected massive PE with obstructive shock
expectedActions.rationale: The constellation is classic. Low EtCO2 (22) in the setting of tachypnea is a marker of poor pulmonary perfusion and suggests obstructive shock.

expectedActions.action: Administer judicious IV fluid bolus 500 mL NS — avoid over-loading the failing right ventricle
expectedActions.rationale: In PE, the RV is failing against high pulmonary pressures. Too much fluid worsens RV distension and further impairs LV filling. Small boluses with frequent reassessment.

expectedActions.action: Early hospital notification — this patient may need thrombolytics or catheter-directed therapy; destination should be a PE-capable center

phases.name: Obstructive Shock Management
phases.description: Patient remains hypotensive despite initial fluid bolus. Crew escalates to push-dose epinephrine or vasopressor infusion while continuing supportive care and expedited transport.

transitions.condition: Patient deteriorates to pulseless electrical activity

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Still tachypneic, clear lungs, hypoxemic despite NRB

expectedActions.action: Initiate Norepinephrine 0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min IV infusion titrated to MAP ≥65 mmHg (preferred vasopressor in obstructive shock)
If norepinephrine infusion not immediately available, administer push-dose epinephrine 10 mcg IV every 3-5 minutes (dilute 1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL in 9 mL NS for 10 mcg/mL)
Consider a second 500 mL NS bolus — reassess lung sounds and BP response carefully to avoid RV overload
Avoid intubation if possible — positive pressure ventilation worsens RV preload and can precipitate arrest; use NIV as a bridge if mental status allows
expectedActions.rationale: In massive PE, intubation is high-risk because of the hemodynamic collapse at induction. Support the BP first, consider NIV, and intubate only if absolutely needed.

expectedActions.action: Expedited transport to a facility with thrombolytics, IR, or ECMO capability; ongoing ED notification

phases.name: Branch: PEA Arrest from Obstructive Shock
phases.description: Patient deteriorates to pulseless electrical activity — typical end pattern for massive PE. Standard ACLS resuscitation with specific attention to PE as the cause; consider thrombolysis on-scene only if protocol allows.

otherFindings: EtCO2 very low confirms obstructive physiology and poor return
PEA with narrow complex consistent with PE

expectedActions.action: Advanced airway as indicated; ventilate 10 breaths/min
Administer Epinephrine 1 mg (1:10,000) IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
Consider reversible causes (Hs and Ts) — this is a thrombosis arrest (PE); contact receiving facility for thrombolytic guidance if protocol allows
Prolonged CPR (60+ minutes) is reasonable in witnessed PE arrest if there is a reversal plan at the destination

criticalActions: Recognize the pattern: postpartum, sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxemia, hypotension, clear lungs, low EtCO2 = PE
Obtain 12-lead EKG for right heart strain
Administer judicious fluid bolus 500 mL NS with reassessment
Initiate vasopressor (norepinephrine infusion or push-dose epinephrine) for shock
Expedited transport to a facility with advanced PE therapies
expectedActions: Apply cardiac monitor and waveform capnography
If deterioration to arrest: high-quality CPR and consider prolonged resuscitation
bonusActions: Articulate low EtCO2 as a marker of obstructive shock
Articulate why positive pressure ventilation is dangerous in PE
Choose norepinephrine over other pressors per protocol

learningObjectives: Recognize massive PE as obstructive shock
Apply MATC Shock protocol for fluid and vasopressor management
Understand why positive pressure ventilation can precipitate arrest in PE
Identify PE risk factors (postpartum, immobility, pregnancy)
Use 12-lead EKG findings to support PE diagnosis
discussionQuestions: Why is EtCO2 low in PE despite tachypnea?
Why is positive pressure ventilation dangerous in massive PE?
What are the 12-lead findings that support PE?
How much fluid is appropriate before starting pressors in obstructive shock?
What risk factors made this patient's PE predictable?
commonPitfalls: Giving large fluid volumes and worsening RV failure
Intubating too early and causing peri-intubation cardiac arrest
Missing the obstructive shock pattern because 'lungs are clear'
Forgetting to ask about postpartum or recent travel/surgery
keyTakeaways: Clear lungs + hypoxemia + shock + low EtCO2 = think PE
Small fluid boluses with reassessment — not 30 mL/kg
Norepinephrine is the pressor of choice
Avoid intubation if possible; NIV is the bridge
